- prompt files exist relative to flow dir; warn on unused files in prompts/
- enum fields: action (issue|comment), submit (new-run|follow-up), waitFor, agents subset of known registry
- semantic: input[].step references an EARLIER step id (forward/self refs are errors -> cycle-impossible by construction); submit:follow-up requires an input step that produced a runner; action:comment requires an input source; agents non-empty after defaults merge
- defaults merge correctness (step overrides flow defaults)
- warn: step count 1 with waitFor set (pointless), missing title/description (picker UX degrades)

COMMAND: nax lint [flow] — all flows when omitted; renders the same plan boxes as preview-boxes on success; --json list of diagnostics; exit 1 on any error-level finding.

NOTE: validation must load flows through the existing configorama path so JS/TS/TOML/JSON flows are validated post-evaluation, same as runtime sees them.

ACCEPTANCE: seeding each defect above into a fixture flow yields exactly one targeted diagnostic naming the path (steps[2].input[0].step) and a fix hint.

title: Pre-run fail-fast: validate every flow before any submission
description:
WHAT: handleRun calls flow-schema validate immediately after flow load, before transport detection, context building, or ANY issue/runner creation. Error-level findings abort with the lint-style report ('flow review-custom failed validation, fix or run nax lint review-custom'). Warn-level findings print but proceed. --dry also runs validation (so dry-run is a real preflight).

Also validate at picker time: flows failing validation appear in the picker greyed/flagged '(invalid: 2 errors)' rather than hidden — discoverable but not runnable.

WHY SEPARATE BEAD: wiring is small but behavioral surface (picker, dry-run, run) needs its own tests and could regress UX if rushed in with the validator itself.

ACCEPTANCE: nax run against a flow with a missing prompt file exits before creating anything, with the same diagnostic nax lint shows; credits spent = 0.

title: nax flow new: interactive flow scaffolder
description:
WHAT: nax flow new <id> generates a project-local flow under the first configured flowsDir (default .github/nax-flows/<id>/).

INTERACTIVE (clack): title, description, number of steps, per step: title, action, submit, agents (multiselect), whether it consumes prior results. Non-interactive: --steps 3 --agents claude,codex --yes produces a sensible review-shaped template.

OUTPUT: flow.yml (with comments explaining each key inline — the generated file doubles as schema documentation) + prompts/N_<step-id>.md stubs each containing a heading, a TODO marker, and the structured-output instruction block matching the findings.json contract so custom flows are findings-compatible by default.

GUARANTEE: generated flow passes nax lint with zero errors/warnings (enforced in tests). If <id> collides with bundled flow, warn that project flow will shadow it (that is the documented precedence) and require --force.

ACCEPTANCE: nax flow new conversion-audit --steps 2 --yes && nax lint conversion-audit -> clean; flow appears in picker under project workflows.

title: Tests: schema validator matrix, fail-fast wiring, scaffold round-trip
description:
WHAT: tests/unit/flow-schema.test.js + flow-new.test.js + scenarios in flows.test.js.

VALIDATOR MATRIX: one fixture per defect class (12+ cases from the lint bead list); assert diagnostic count, level, path string, fix hint presence. Valid bundled flows: assert ALL 14 bundled flows pass with zero errors (this doubles as a regression net for bundled flow edits — a failing bundled flow should fail CI).

FAIL-FAST: run path with invalid flow -> no gh/runner calls attempted (assert via exec seam call log), exit code 1, stderr contains diagnostic; warn-only flow proceeds; picker shows invalid marker.

SCAFFOLD: generate in tmp across formats default, then lint -> zero diagnostics; round-trip load through configorama equals expected structure; collision without --force refused.

LOGGING: diagnostics printed verbatim on failure.

title: Nax Visualize: React Flow workflow UI and local operator cockpit
description:
WHY: Nax workflows are currently discoverable and previewable in the terminal, but the mental model is graph-shaped: steps fan out to agents, gate on results, feed later steps, and produce durable artifacts. A local UI can make that workflow structure inspectable at a glance and eventually become the safest operator surface for dry-runs, real runs, live status, and artifact handoff.

GOAL: Add `nax visualize` as a local browser workbench for workflow discovery, React Flow graph rendering, dry-run preview, run submission, live status, and artifact browsing. The first shippable increment must be read-only and low-risk: list workflows from the same loader as the CLI and render selected workflows as React Flow nodes/edges. Later increments add privileged run control through a localhost API with explicit safety boundaries.

CONTEXT: Existing source of truth is `src/flows.js` (`listFlows`, `loadFlow`, `loadStepPrompt`). The terminal preview lives in `bin/nax.js` via `printFlowPlan`. Actual orchestration is currently entangled in `handleRun()` inside `bin/nax.js`, so supported browser-triggered runs eventually need orchestration extracted into reusable modules. The package is CommonJS and currently advertises Node >=18, while Vite 8 tooling requires Node 20.19+ or 22.12+; end users should not need Vite at runtime if built assets ship in npm.

NON-NEGOTIABLES: The browser never calls Netlify or GitHub directly. The visualize server binds to localhost by default, validates workflow ids and options, uses argv arrays instead of shell strings for delegated commands, and protects mutating endpoints with a per-process token. The UI should be a dense operational workbench, not a landing page.

PHASING: Phase 1 read-only visualizer; Phase 2 dry-run/command preview; Phase 3 real run submission with streamed events; Phase 4 reusable orchestration extraction; Phase 5 artifact/run browser.
close_reason: Completed Nax Visualize React Flow UI graph: workflow discovery, React Flow rendering, dry-run preview, safe run control, artifact/run browsing, direct runner extraction, tests, docs, and validation.

title: Resolve visualize product and safety defaults before run-control work
description:
WHAT: Resolve the open product questions before implementing run-control defaults: whether `nax visualize` help text is marked experimental, whether the first run button is dry-run only, whether real runs require an extra flag or explicit UI confirmation, whether the UI should auto-open latest unfinished workflow, whether workflow editing is a future goal or explicitly out of scope, and whether branch selection defaults to existing `resolveWorkflowBranch()` logic.

WHY: These choices shape safety, UX, and implementation boundaries. Deciding them in plan space avoids a UI that accidentally normalizes expensive remote actions.

ACCEPTANCE: Decisions are recorded in bead notes or implementation docs and reflected in command help/UI copy. Real-run implementation beads can proceed without ambiguity about default behavior.
notes: Decisions for implementation: mark `nax visualize` experimental for the current release; dry-run remains the primary visible action; real runs are allowed only after an explicit browser confirmation that shows workflow, project root, branch, transport, selected models, and remote side effects; all mutating endpoints require the per-process visualize token; workflow editing is out of scope for this graph; branch defaults remain a simple UI value for now, with CLI-side `resolveWorkflowBranch()` still authoritative when a run is invoked.
close_reason: Recorded safety/product defaults for visualize run-control: experimental command, dry-run primary, confirmed real runs, token-protected mutations, no workflow editing, CLI branch resolution remains authoritative.

title: Document full visualize UI, run-control, and artifact browser
description:
WHAT: Document the full visualize surface after dry-run, real-run, and artifact browser phases land. Update README command sections, examples, safety notes, troubleshooting, and any docs/ai references. Include `nax visualize`, dry-run from UI, real-run confirmation, token-protected localhost API behavior at a high level, artifact browser, and Node/Vite version expectations for development.

WHY: Once the UI can create remote side effects, stale or underspecified docs become a safety problem. Users need to understand what runs locally, what talks to Netlify/GitHub, and how to recover artifacts.

ACCEPTANCE: README/docs match shipped behavior for read-only, dry-run, real-run, and artifact browser surfaces. Troubleshooting includes missing built assets, port conflicts, bad token, invalid workflow, and missing Netlify/GitHub prerequisites. Existing docs wave can reference or absorb this bead if desired.
close_reason: Documented visualize workflow list, React Flow graph, dry-run, run submission, recent artifacts, token behavior, Vite tooling, and troubleshooting.

title: Documentation wave: README + docs for all new surface area
description:
WHAT: The README is the product (35KB, carefully structured: TL;DR, comparison table, flow anatomy, troubleshooting). Every epic in this graph adds user-facing surface that must land there with the same quality bar, and stale docs are worse than no docs.

SCOPE per shipped epic/feature (check off as each lands — this bead stays open as the docs gate until the graph completes):
- Commands table: doctor, usage, clean, watch, schedule, flow new, lint, handoff --to targets
- Flags table: --json, --base, --var, --max-credits, --notify-url, --retry-attempts, --no-lock
- Flow Anatomy: inputs:, when:, onFailure:/maxRetries: keys with the same key-table treatment as existing step keys; flow-schema as the authoritative reference
- New sections: 'Cost' (usage + budgets + estimates), 'Findings pipeline' (findings.json schema + handoff targets diagram), 'Scheduled flows'
- Troubleshooting table: rows for every new failure mode introduced (lock refusal, budget abort, validation failure, webhook delivery warning) — each row maps to a doctor check where applicable
- Limitations section: REMOVE lines this graph fixes (three-agents hardcoded, macOS-only notifications, sync discovery) as they ship — stale limitations erode trust in the whole list
- docs/ai/: findings.json schema doc, transcript recording how-to, flow-schema reference generated or hand-written

QUALITY BAR: match existing README voice (terse, table-driven, no marketing fluff); every code fence language-tagged per repo markdown rules.

title: Webhook notifications: --notify-url + failure notices
description:
WHAT: Generalize notifications beyond macOS osascript:
- --notify-url <url> (+ nax.config.json notifyUrl, env NAX_NOTIFY_URL): POST JSON { event: workflow-completed|workflow-failed|workflow-interrupted|step-completed, runId, flowId, flowTitle, branch, status, durationMs, usage: { totalCredits }, summaryPath, links } on terminal events (step-completed only with --notify-steps, off by default to avoid spam).
- Payload is Slack-incoming-webhook compatible when the URL host matches hooks.slack.com (wrap as text blocks); otherwise raw JSON. Discord similar simple detection. Keep detection trivial — two well-known hosts, everything else gets the raw shape.
- Existing --notify (osascript) unchanged and composable with --notify-url.
- FAILURES NOTIFY TOO: today only completion is celebrated; interrupted/failed runs are precisely when a away-from-keyboard user needs the ping.
- Delivery: 5s timeout, one retry, failure to deliver logs a warning and never affects run status or exit code.

WHY: long fan-out runs (25-45 min) are exactly the start-and-walk-away workload; non-Mac users and remote/CI usage currently have zero signal.

TESTS (tests/unit/notify.test.js): payload schema per event, slack/discord wrapping by host, timeout + retry-once behavior against a real local http server fixture (node http, not mocked transport), run exit code unaffected by sink failure, step-notify gating flag.

ACCEPTANCE: nax review --notify-url https://hooks.slack.com/... pings on completion AND on ctrl-c interrupt.

title: PR review output: post consensus findings as a PR review
description:
WHAT: nax handoff --to pr-review [--pr 123]: take findings.json from the selected workflow and post a single GitHub PR review (gh api) containing: summary comment (consensus overview + workflow link) + one inline comment per finding that maps to a file/line in the PR diff (findings carry files[]; line mapping best-effort via diff hunk search for the finding's quoted code when present, else file-level comment). Findings not mappable to the diff appended to the summary body under 'Outside this diff'.

DEFAULT PR: if the workflow was run with --branch '#123' that PR; else the open PR for the workflow branch; else require --pr.

IDEMPOTENCY: review body carries marker '<!-- nax-review:<workflowRunId> -->'; existing marker -> refuse with link unless --force (which posts a fresh review, never edits).

WHY: closes the loop for PR-centric teams — multi-agent consensus lands exactly where humans already review, instead of a side-channel issue. Event-comment mode (review type COMMENT, never REQUEST_CHANGES) keeps nax advisory, not gatekeeping — that is a deliberate posture decision.

TESTS: handoff-targets tests extend: payload shape (review body, inline comment file/line), unmappable-finding fallback, idempotency refusal, default-PR resolution chain. gh exec seam with fixture payloads captured from real gh output.

ACCEPTANCE: after nax review --branch '#123', nax handoff --to pr-review posts one review with inline comments on the PR; rerun refuses politely.

title: Visualize Phase 3: safe real run submission from UI
description: Enable real workflow submission from the UI through a token-protected localhost API. This phase may initially delegate to `node bin/nax.js run ...` with argv arrays, streams stdout/stderr/process lifecycle through server-sent events, guards duplicate submissions, and recovers visible state from `.nax/` after refresh.
close_reason: Completed Phase 3 safe real run submission from UI with token guardrails, subprocess run endpoint, SSE/cancel, confirmation UI, state recovery, and tests.

title: Add token-protected mutating API guardrails
description:
WHAT: Harden the visualize server for mutating run-control endpoints. Require a per-process token on every POST/DELETE-style endpoint, validate origin/host assumptions appropriate for localhost, bind to `127.0.0.1` by default, and keep all workflow ids/options validated against loaded workflows.

WHY: Real run submission can create GitHub issues, trigger GitHub Actions, and spend Netlify agent credits. Localhost is still a privileged control plane and must be treated accordingly.

ACCEPTANCE: Mutating requests without token fail. Read-only routes remain accessible for the loaded UI. Workflow id, transport, branch, model, step, and from-step validation happens before any child process or runner action. Tests cover missing token, bad token, and valid token paths.
close_reason: Mutating visualize endpoints now require the per-process token; server validates workflow ids, transports, step/fromStep, and model names before spawning commands.

title: Add safe subprocess-backed real workflow run endpoint
description:
WHAT: Add `POST /api/workflows/:id/runs` using safe subprocess delegation as the first real-run implementation. Build argv arrays directly for `node bin/nax.js run <flowId> --project-root <root> --transport <transport> --branch <branch> --force ...`; never accept or execute arbitrary shell command strings.

DETAILS: Record process id, run request id, workflow id, command args, startedAt, exitedAt, exit code, stdout/stderr buffers or event references, and current status. Integrate with existing run-lock and fail-fast validation work when available so duplicate/corrupt runs are avoided before remote side effects happen.

ACCEPTANCE: A real run can be started from the UI in a wired repo. Failed process start and nonzero exit both return clear lifecycle state. The endpoint refuses unsupported transports/options before spawning.
close_reason: Added POST /api/workflows/:id/runs backed by safe argv-array subprocess delegation, active run registry, duplicate active workflow guard, and command lifecycle metadata.

title: Stream run lifecycle events and support cancellation
description:
WHAT: Add server-sent events for run lifecycle: stdout chunks, stderr chunks, process started, process exited, run-state refresh, and error events. Add cancellation endpoint for still-running child processes with clear semantics and status reporting.

WHY: Long agent workflows need live feedback. A browser refresh should not be the only way to know whether the run is still alive, and operators need a controlled cancel path for accidental submissions.

ACCEPTANCE: UI can subscribe to `/api/runs/:runId/events` and receive ordered lifecycle events. Cancel sends a termination signal, updates status, and reports if the process was already complete. Event streams close cleanly at terminal state.
close_reason: Added /api/runs/:id/events SSE replay/live stream and /api/runs/:id/cancel endpoint with event recording and client cleanup.

title: Add confirmed real-run UI and duplicate submission guard
description:
WHAT: Add UI real-run controls behind an explicit confirmation flow. The confirmation must show workflow title/id, project root, branch, transport, selected models, context presence, and the fact that the run can create remote work and spend credits. Prevent accidental duplicate submissions for the same active workflow unless explicitly confirmed.

WHY: The UI should make starting a real workflow easier but not casual. Dry-run remains the safer default action.

ACCEPTANCE: Real run button is visually distinct from dry-run. Confirmation modal summarizes exact options. Duplicate active run attempts are blocked or require a second explicit confirmation. Lifecycle events update the run panel.
close_reason: Added confirmed real-run UI button separate from dry-run; confirmation names branch, transport, models, and remote credit side effects; output streams into a run panel.

title: Recover run status from .nax workflow state
description:
WHAT: Read `.nax/workflows` state after run submission and on browser refresh. Expose run list/detail APIs sufficient for the UI to recover active or recently completed run status from durable state instead of only in-memory child process records.

DETAILS: Use existing `listRunStates`, `workflowStatePath`, and artifact helper semantics where possible. Distinguish in-memory active process status from durable workflow status. Link to workflow summary artifact when present.

ACCEPTANCE: Refreshing the browser during or after a run can show known run status and artifact paths. Completed/failed/interrupted states display correctly when read only from `.nax/`.
close_reason: Added /api/runs and /api/runs/:id durable state reads from .nax/workflows with public run-state serialization.

title: Test token-protected run control, SSE, cancel, and recovery
description:
WHAT: Test real-run control without requiring real Netlify or GitHub side effects in CI. Cover token enforcement, argv construction, process lifecycle capture, SSE event ordering, cancellation, duplicate guard behavior, and refresh recovery from fixture `.nax/workflows` state.

DETAILS: Use local child-process fixtures or injected spawn seams. Tests should assert that user inputs are never shell-concatenated and that no remote command is attempted for invalid inputs.

ACCEPTANCE: Server tests exercise start, event stream, cancel, process failure, and state recovery paths. Failure output includes enough lifecycle details to diagnose broken run-control behavior.
close_reason: Added run-control tests for token enforcement, run start, event replay, cancel response, and durable .nax workflow state recovery; reran browser smoke.

title: Pluggable agent registry: config-driven agent list beyond claude/gemini/codex
description:
WHAT: Today the agent set is hardcoded in multiple places (flow defaults, validation, title casing, flavor messages, runner mention building). README Limitations admits it: 'Three agents. Adding more requires extending the runner action and the flow schema.' Centralize into src/agent-registry.js:
- built-ins: claude, gemini, codex with displayName, mention/model identifier per transport, flavor messages key
- nax.config.json extension: agents: { grok: { displayName: 'Grok', runnerModel: '...', transports: [netlify-api] } } — validated at load (unknown fields rejected, transports subset checked)
- flow-schema validates step agents against the merged registry, so a project that registers an agent can use it in project flows immediately; bundled flows keep using built-ins only
- doctor gains a check: each registered agent's transport support vs configured transports (warn if an agent is unusable everywhere)

HONESTY GUARD: nax cannot make the Netlify runner support a model it does not support — registry validation should verify the runner accepts the model identifier where the API allows discovery, else mark the agent 'unverified, will fail at submit if unsupported' in doctor output. This bead is the CLI-side plumbing so that the day the runner adds a model, users add 4 lines of config instead of waiting for a nax release.

TESTS (tests/unit/agent-registry.test.js): merge precedence (config cannot redefine built-ins without explicit override: true), validation rejections with messages, flow-schema integration (project flow with registered agent lints clean; unknown agent names the registry as fix), title/mention building for a registered agent, doctor check outputs.

ACCEPTANCE: register a hypothetical agent in config, reference it in a project flow, nax lint passes, dry-run plan renders it; removing config makes lint fail with a clear message.

title: Harden prompt delivery: file-based prompts + tiered context compaction
description:
WHY: The #1 documented bug (docs/ai/bugs/netlify-agent-runner-argument-list-too-long.md) is follow-up sessions failing at runner launch because large prompts (prior-round results embedded) travel via argv/env and blow OS ARG_MAX. Separately, the github-actions transport enforces an ~80KB env string budget at plan time and just errors, forcing users to hand-trim context. Both are symptoms of one root cause: prompt size handling is ad-hoc and transport-specific.

GOAL: One shared prompt-budget module with progressive degradation, plus a root-cause delivery fix so big prompts never hit argv limits on the netlify-api transport.

DESIGN:
- New src/prompt-budget.js: knows each transport's hard limit (gh env ~80KB safe max, runner argv limit), measures a built prompt, and applies compaction tiers in order until it fits: tier 0 = full; tier 1 = structured-findings-only from prior rounds (drop prose); tier 2 = links-only ledger (issue URLs + one-line summaries, instruct agent to fetch); tier 3 = hard error with exact byte counts and the flag to fix it.
- netlify-api transport: deliver prompt content via a file written to the repo checkout (or stdin) instead of argv/env where the runner API supports it; prompt text becomes a pointer + payload.
- Both transports report which tier was applied in console output and in workflow.json (context.compactionTier) so results are interpretable later.

ACCEPTANCE: A review flow whose round-2 prompt embeds >200KB of round-1 results completes on both transports without manual trimming; the applied tier is visible in run output and artifacts.

SERVES: reliability pillar — 'Resume over re-run' philosophy extends to 'degrade over die'.

title: Build src/prompt-budget.js shared budget + compaction tier module
File-based prompt delivery for netlify-api follow-up runs
description:
WHAT: Root-cause fix for the argument-list-too-long failure. When submitting follow-up sessions through the Netlify Agent Runner API (src/local-runner.js submitLocalAgentRun), stop passing the full prompt through argv/env once it exceeds a conservative threshold (~100KB). Instead write the full prompt body to a temp artifact the runner can read, and submit a short pointer prompt: 'Your full instructions are in <path>; read that file first and follow it exactly.'

INVESTIGATE FIRST: confirm what channels the runner API actually supports for large payloads (request body field limits vs argv pass-through into the runner process). The bug doc notes the failure is at runner process launch, i.e., the orchestrator-side submission succeeds but spawning chokes — so the fix likely lives in HOW the action invokes the agent CLI, and our lever is keeping the submitted prompt small. If we cannot change the runner side, the pointer-file approach via a committed-or-pushed context file (or issue body, which has no argv problem) is the fallback: for action=comment steps the content already lives in the GitHub comment, so the runner prompt can reference the comment URL instead of embedding.

ACCEPTANCE: the repro in docs/ai/bugs/netlify-agent-runner-argument-list-too-long.md no longer fails; a follow-up step with 300KB of embedded prior results launches successfully.

DEPENDS: uses prompt-budget module to decide when to switch delivery modes.

title: Wire prompt-budget tiers into both transports + retry path
description:
WHAT: Replace plan-time 80KB error in bin/nax.js (github-actions path) and the retry-time prompt compaction in src/local-runner.js with calls to prompt-budget fitPrompt. Record the applied tier into run-state (workflow.json context.compactionTier and per-run promptTier) via src/run-state.js, and surface it in console output ('context compacted to tier 1: dropped 142KB of prior-round prose') and in workflow summary.md via src/workflow-artifacts.js.

UX: warnings should name exactly what was dropped and how to override (--no-fetch-results, --pr-limit N, --context-file). Never silently truncate mid-section — tiers drop whole named sections only.

ACCEPTANCE: oversized prompt on github-actions degrades to tier 1/2 with a clear console line instead of erroring; identical compaction behavior on first submit and on retry.

title: Tests: prompt-budget unit + oversized-prompt integration coverage
description:
WHAT: Comprehensive tests with detailed logging so post-implementation verification is trivial.

UNIT (tests/unit/prompt-budget.test.js, node --test like existing suite):
- tier 0 pass-through is byte-identical for under-budget prompts
- tier 1 drops exactly the sections marked droppable, preserves structured findings blocks
- tier 2 produces links-only ledger containing every source issue URL
- tier 3 throws with byte counts and remediation hints in the message (assert message content)
- boundary cases: exactly at safeMax, 1 byte over, empty sections, unicode multi-byte counting

INTEGRATION (extend tests/unit/flow-execution.test.js patterns): drive a fake flow whose round-1 results are generated >200KB strings and assert (a) the built round-2 submission is under the transport budget, (b) workflow.json records compactionTier, (c) console output (captured) names the dropped sections. Use real generated data, not mocks of the budget logic itself.

LOGGING: each test logs input sizes, chosen tier, output sizes so failures are diagnosable from CI output alone.

title: Visualize Phase 1: read-only React Flow workflow demo
description: Build the first safe demo: `nax visualize [workflow]` starts a local server, opens a Vite 8 + React 19 + React Flow app, lists all workflows from the existing loader, and renders the selected workflow as deterministic step nodes and dependency edges. This phase deliberately avoids remote side effects and real run submission.
close_reason: Completed Phase 1 read-only visualizer: toolchain, graph transform, localhost API, CLI command, Vite React Flow workbench, static packaging, tests, browser smoke, and README documentation.

title: Visualize UI tooling: add Vite 8, React 19, React Flow deps and scripts
description:
WHAT: Add the frontend/tooling dependencies and scripts required for the visualize UI while keeping the existing CommonJS CLI usable. Add root package dependencies/devDependencies for Vite 8, React 19, React DOM 19, @xyflow/react 12, and the React Vite plugin. Add scripts `visualize:dev`, `visualize:build`, and `visualize:preview` pointing at `web/vite.config.mjs`.

WHY: Root dependencies avoid workspace/release churn for the first UI. The UI source remains isolated under `web/`, but a global install can still ship prebuilt assets later.

CONSTRAINTS: Do not raise the published CLI runtime casually. Document that developing/building the UI needs Node 20.19+ or 22.12+ because Vite 8 requires it, while the built CLI should remain installable for the current engine policy if possible. Avoid adding a separate package manager or workspace unless a later bead proves it necessary.

ACCEPTANCE: `npm install` updates lockfile cleanly; `npm run visualize:build` has a real script target once the web app lands; existing `npm run check` and `npm test` remain callable.
close_reason: Added Vite 8, React 19, React Flow, and plugin-react dev tooling plus visualize dev/build/preview scripts.

title: Document experimental nax visualize Phase 1 usage
description:
WHAT: Update README and/or command help with a short experimental `nax visualize` section. Include purpose, prerequisites, example commands, and honest scope: Phase 1 is read-only workflow visualization, not workflow editing or browser-triggered remote execution.

DETAILS: Mention `nax visualize review`, `--no-open`, `--project-root`, and the dev/build scripts. Note that UI development/building uses Vite 8 and therefore needs Node 20.19+ or 22.12+, while published built assets should avoid requiring Vite at runtime.

WHY: Users should understand what the command does and does not do, especially before run buttons are introduced.

ACCEPTANCE: README has a concise section matching the existing direct style. Help text marks the command experimental if that remains the product decision.
close_reason: Added README command entry and experimental nax visualize section covering examples, read-only Phase 1 scope, workflow loader behavior, no side effects, and Vite/Node build requirement.

title: Add browser smoke coverage for React Flow visualizer
description:
WHAT: Add browser smoke/e2e coverage for the Phase 1 UI once the app can build. Start the visualize server against a temp project, open the UI in a real browser automation tool or lightweight equivalent, verify workflows load, select Review, assert the canvas is nonblank, assert three nodes/two edges are visible, and capture desktop plus narrow viewport screenshots for debugging.

DETAILS: The test should catch blank React Flow canvases, broken asset serving, text overlap severe enough to hide content, and failure to load selected workflow from URL. Keep this as smoke coverage, not exhaustive visual regression.

WHY: React Flow issues often fail visually even when API tests pass. A real browser smoke test is the guardrail that `nax visualize review` actually produces a usable UI.

ACCEPTANCE: CI or local test script can run the smoke test with detailed logs. On failure it leaves screenshots or clear console output. The test verifies desktop and mobile-ish/narrow layouts.
close_reason: Added Playwright smoke test that starts the visualize server, opens Review, verifies three workflow nodes and two React Flow edges on desktop and narrow viewports, and captures screenshots as attachments.

title: Build pure flow-to-React-Flow graph transform
description:
WHAT: Implement `src/visualize-graph.js`, a pure CommonJS transform that converts a normalized Nax flow into React Flow-compatible nodes, edges, and metadata. Inputs should support `{ flow, selectedAgents, runState }`; output should include stable `nodes`, `edges`, and `metadata`.

DETAILS: Node data must carry `kind: workflow-step`, `stepId`, index, title, description, action, submit, waitFor, agents, input, status, and sourceLabel. Edges come from explicit `step.input[].step` references first. If a later step has no explicit input and the flow is linear, add a sequential edge from the previous runnable step. Use stable edge ids like `edge:<sourceStepId>:<targetStepId>`. Mark follow-up edges or nodes so the UI can visually distinguish follow-up sessions from new agent runs.

WHY: The graph transform is the reusable contract between the CLI/server and React Flow. Keeping it pure makes it easy to unit test, easy to reuse in JSON APIs, and independent from any future layout engine.

ACCEPTANCE: Review flow produces 3 nodes and 2 edges. Explicit `input` dependencies override sequential assumptions. Agent filtering updates node agents without mutating the original flow. The function is deterministic for stable snapshot tests.
close_reason: Implemented pure flowToGraph transform with agent filtering, run-state overlay, deterministic layout, input/sequential edges, and transitive edge reduction; added focused unit coverage.

title: Add read-only localhost visualize API server
description:
WHAT: Add `src/visualize-server.js` and any small API helper module needed to serve read-only visualize data over a localhost HTTP server. Initial routes: `GET /api/health`, `GET /api/workflows`, `GET /api/workflows/:id`, and `GET /api/workflows/:id/graph`.

DETAILS: Use Node built-in `http` initially. Resolve workflows through existing `listFlows()`/`loadFlow()` using the same projectRoot/flowsDir semantics as `nax list`. Return stable JSON errors with status, code, and message. Bind to `127.0.0.1` by default. Generate a per-process token and include it in server metadata even though Phase 1 has no mutating endpoints, so the security shape is ready before run buttons exist.

WHY: The UI must not duplicate filesystem/config loading in browser code. The local server is the boundary where CLI-aware project context becomes browser-safe JSON.

ACCEPTANCE: A test can start the server on an ephemeral port, fetch health, list workflows, and fetch the Review graph. Unknown workflow ids return structured 404 JSON. Project-local flows appear before bundled flows, matching existing loader behavior.
close_reason: Added read-only localhost visualize API server with health, workflows, workflow detail, and workflow graph routes; live probe returns Review graph as 3 nodes and 2 edges.

title: Add nax visualize [workflow] command and server lifecycle
description:
WHAT: Add `nax visualize [workflow]` to Commander wiring. Options: `--project-root <path>`, repeatable `--flows-dir <path>`, `--host <host>` default `127.0.0.1`, `--port <port>` default auto, `--no-open`, and `--dev` for local Vite development integration.

DETAILS: Resolve project root with the same helper as list/run. Start the visualize server, open the browser using the existing `open` dependency unless `--no-open` is set, encode the initial workflow id in the URL, print URL and project root, and keep the process alive until interrupted. Do not run workflow execution in Phase 1.

WHY: This is the user-facing entry point and should feel like a natural Nax command, not a separate web app the user needs to discover.

ACCEPTANCE: `node bin/nax.js visualize review --no-open` starts the server and prints a local URL without opening a browser. Invalid initial workflow ids surface clearly. `--flows-dir` and `--project-root` affect the workflow list the same way they do for `nax list`.
close_reason: Added nax visualize [workflow] command with project-root, flows-dir, host, port, no-open, and dev options; spawned smoke confirms URL/project-root output.

title: Create Vite React workbench shell and API client
description:
WHAT: Create the Vite application under `web/` with `index.html`, `vite.config.mjs`, TypeScript config if used, `src/main.tsx`, `src/App.tsx`, API client, shared types, and the operational workbench shell.

LAYOUT: Left sidebar for workflow list/search/source labels, main React Flow canvas, right inspector, top toolbar for project root/refresh/future run controls, and bottom/status area reserved for future dry-run/run streams. The first viewport should be the actual workbench, not a hero or documentation page.

WHY: The initial UI should prove the product direction immediately: workflows are selectable, graph-shaped, and inspectable.

ACCEPTANCE: The app fetches `/api/workflows`, selects the URL workflow or first workflow, handles loading/error/empty states, and does not require any remote service. `npm run visualize:build` compiles once the app and dependencies are present.
close_reason: Created Vite React workbench shell, API client, URL workflow selection, loading/error states, toolbar, and status strip.

title: Implement React Flow canvas and custom workflow step node
description:
WHAT: Implement the React Flow canvas and custom workflow step node using `@xyflow/react` named imports and `@xyflow/react/dist/style.css`. Include `ReactFlowProvider`, `ReactFlow`, `Background`, `Controls`, and optionally `MiniMap` if it helps orientation.

NODE DESIGN: Header shows step number/title and action label. Body shows description. Footer shows agent chips. Include top target and bottom source handles for edge attachment, with handle dots visually hidden by CSS but still present. Use `nodesDraggable={false}`, `nodesConnectable={false}`, selectable elements, `fitView`, and smoothstep default edges for Phase 1.

WHY: This is the visual equivalent of the existing terminal box preview, but with native graph interaction and a path to future live state overlays.

ACCEPTANCE: Review renders as three clear nodes connected vertically. Long titles/descriptions wrap without overlap. Selecting a node updates inspector state. Canvas is nonblank and usable at desktop and narrow widths.
close_reason: Implemented React Flow canvas with custom workflow step nodes, hidden handles, smoothstep edges, controls, minimap, fitView, and responsive styling.

title: Build workflow list, search, and step/workflow inspector
description:
WHAT: Build workflow list, search/filter, and inspector components. The list should show workflow title, id, source label, step count, and model summary. The inspector should show selected workflow metadata by default and selected step metadata when a node is selected.

DETAILS: Reuse normalized fields from the API: description, defaults, source/sourceLabel, steps, agents, action, submit, waitFor, input, prompt path when exposed. Keep the UI dense and operational. Use icon buttons for refresh/open-type commands when practical.

WHY: A graph alone is not enough; operators need to compare workflows, see where a project-local flow came from, and inspect exactly what a selected step will do.

ACCEPTANCE: Search narrows the workflow list. Source labels distinguish project vs bundled workflows. Selecting a workflow refreshes graph and inspector. Selecting a step shows action, submit mode, agents, input dependencies, waitFor, and description.
close_reason: Implemented searchable workflow list and inspector for workflow/step metadata including source labels, step actions, submit modes, agents, inputs, and paths.

title: Serve built UI assets and include them in package files
description:
WHAT: Wire production static asset serving and npm package inclusion for the visualize UI. The server should serve `web/dist` in normal mode after `npm run visualize:build`; dev mode can point operators at the Vite dev server or proxy to it.

DETAILS: Update package `files` so built UI assets are included in published tarballs. Ensure the server can find assets relative to installed package paths, not only the source checkout. Add a clear error when assets are missing that tells developers to run `npm run visualize:build`.

WHY: Global `nax visualize` should work for users without asking them to install Vite or run a dev server.

ACCEPTANCE: After build, `nax visualize --no-open` serves `index.html` and static assets from `web/dist`. A packed npm artifact would include the needed files. Missing assets fail with a helpful message rather than a blank browser.
close_reason: Built UI assets under web/dist, added static serving from visualize server, verified asset responses, and included web/dist in package files.

title: Add Phase 1 graph and read-only API tests
description:
WHAT: Add focused tests for Phase 1: graph transform unit tests and read-only visualize API integration tests.

CASES: Review graph has 3 nodes/2 edges; explicit input dependencies generate correct edges; sequential fallback works when expected; agent filtering changes node data; project-local workflow metadata survives API serialization; unknown workflow id returns structured 404; server health route returns project root/version-like metadata; no mutating endpoint exists yet.

WHY: The UI depends on data correctness more than pixel perfection in Phase 1. The pure graph transform and API routes are where regressions are easiest to catch cheaply.

ACCEPTANCE: Tests run under existing `node --test` patterns. Failures print the graph/API payloads needed to debug. `npm test` and `npm run check` pass.
close_reason: Added graph transform and visualize server tests covering Review graph, sequential fallback, agent filtering, run-state overlay, health/list/detail/graph routes, project flow precedence, 404 errors, and static asset serving.
